title: About
text:
95% of my day starts or ends with a workout. Health is my number 1 priority.
My motto is “bet often, manage risk, learn, iterate”.
My work experience can be summarized as an analyst, currently self-employed and focusing on crypto. Before that I spent a few years in Ecommerce Growth and Corporate Finance.

title: Xin Chào
text: Chào mừng đến với bài viết đầu tiên của tôi trên blog này. Đây là blog cá nhân và tôi sẽ viết về nhiều chủ đề khác nhau. Tuỳ thuộc vào chủ đề mà ngôn ngữ của bài viết cũng sẽ khác nhau, either English hoặc tiếng Việt 😁.

title: Stablecoin Depeg
text:
During the depeg event on Ethereum:

USDT price peaked then Dexs volume peaked then depeg stablecoins price bottomed,
Liquidity flee / Arbitrage peaked on Curve then peaked higher on Uniswap ; from Stable to Non-stable; from USDC to USDT and WETH.
USDT is not replacing USDC as the go-to stablecoin onchain.

title: Stablecoin Depeg
text:
Volume Breakdown

By Dex




                                                





                                                



Uniswap and Curve account for more than 90% of total Dexs volume.
During depeg:

At first, volume on Curve rose faster than on Uniswap (till 1am Mar 11). After that, volume on Uniswap surpassed Curve and peaked when depeged stablecoins made the lowest low.
Curve saw its share in total Dexs volume 4x to 36% from 9%. Meanwhile, Uniswap’s share decreased to 60% from 85%.

➡️ The liquidity move started on Curve first then Uniswap and Other Dexs.
After depeg, Uniswap regained share from Curve.


By Pool Type






Note



Stablecoins identified as all coins in the stablecoin list of Defillama here.






                                                





                                                



During depeg:

Volume in Stable ↔︎️ Stable pools rose faster than in Stable ↔︎️ Non-stable pools when depeged stablecoins price were making lower lows. Volume in the latter peaked after but higher than volume in the former ($846M vs $834M per hour).
When depeged stablecoins price started to recover and made higher highs:

Volume in Stable ↔︎️ Non-stable pools dropped rapidly and stayed below $400M per hour. Meanwhile, volume in Stable ↔︎️ Stable pools was more volatile, dropped and rose rapidly, made other 2 highs above $500M.
Volume in Non-stable ↔︎️ Non-stable pools only peaked at $380M per hour at 9am Mar 13, when USDC and DAI price were about to reclaim $0.99.

Overall, Stable ↔︎️ Stable accounts for more than half total volume, increased to 54% from 16% before depeg. Meanwhile, Stable ↔︎️ Non-stable lost its share in total volume to 38% from 60% before depeg.

➡️ The liquidity move started in Stable ↔︎️ Stable pools first then in Stable ↔︎️ Non-stable, and Non-stable ↔︎️ Non-stable pools.
After depeg, Stable ↔︎️ Non-stable pools regained share from Stable ↔︎️ Stable pools.


By Swap-to Coins




                                                





                                                



Top 4 Swap-to Coins including USDC, USDT, WETH, and DAI account for as high as 91% (during depeg) in total Dexs volume.
During depeg:

Before 2am Mar 11, when USDC and DAI price were about to breakdown $0.99, volume swap-to USDT was as high if not higher than volume swap-to USDC.
When the prices broke down $0.99, volume swap-to USDC and DAI rose higher while volume swap-to USDT dropped.
3 stablecoins USDC, USDT, and DAI increased their share in total Dexs volume. Meanwhile, WETH lost the most share to 21% from 36%.

➡️ Liquidity moved to USDT first then to USDC and DAI when their prices broke down $0.99.
After depeg, USDT gained share from USDC in total Dexs volume, increased to 16% from 12%. Meanwhile, USDC’s share decreased to 29% from 34%. However, recently, USDC has been regaining share, see Section 3.3.
Next, let’s examine net liquidity in-flow in each period to see which coins did traders flee to.



                                                


During depeg:

USDT and WETH saw the most net liquidity in-flow, added $309M and $232M respectively.
USDC and Other Coins saw the most net liquidity out-flow, lost $159M and $423M respectively.

After the depeg, liquidity flow to USDT, WETH, and USDC reversed.
➡️ During depeg, before depeg stablecoins price broke down $0.99, the liquidity move started on Curve first (then on Uniswap and other Dexs), where traders fled to USDT from those stablecoins in Stable ↔︎️ Stable pools. When stablecoins price broke down $0.99, traders partially swapped back to those stablecoins and partially swapped to non-stablecoins. When depeg stablecoins price bottomed and then started to recover, liquidity was mostly moved between Stable ↔︎️ Stable, and Stable ↔︎️ Non-stable pools.

title: Stablecoin Depeg
text:
Liquidity Flows






Note



If not specify, liquidity flow of a coin in this section is total of both directions flow-in/out.



Uniswap




                                                



                                                



                                                



Before depeg, USDC ↔︎️ WETH was the biggest flow with $2.1B, and more than double the second biggest flow, which was WETH ↔︎️ Other Coins. Meanwhile, USDC ↔︎️ USDT was the third biggest flow with $476M.
During depeg:

Uniswap saw the rise of Stable ↔︎️ Stable pools, specifically is the USDC ↔︎️ USDT flow.
USDC ↔︎️ WETH was still the biggest flow with $7.2B (~3x vs Before depeg). However the flow which increased the most was USDC ↔︎️ USDT with $6.3B (~13x vs Before depeg).
Other noticable increases were USDT ↔︎️ WETH: added $2.8B from $0; USDC ↔︎️ DAI: 43x to $1.5B from $35M.

After depeg, the flows looked similar to before depeg.


Curve




                                                



                                                



                                                




During depeg:

Unlike Uniswap, on Curve, Stable ↔︎️ Stable pools are dominant all the time, mostly attributed to USDC ↔︎️ USDT flow.
The main difference vs before depeg was flows to and from DAI, which replaced WETH as the third most volume swap-to and swap-from coin.
Total liquidity flow in/out DAI 38x to $3.4B from $90M before depeg.


After depeg, WETH reclaimed the third posistion.


Other Dexs




                                                



                                                



                                                



Since depeg began, bb-a-USDC and bb-a-DAI (Balancer Boosted Aave stable coins) have de-throned WETH, and replaced USDC in liquidity flow on Other Dexs.

title: Stablecoin Depeg
section: Is USDT the new go-to stablecoin in Defi?
text:
Is USDT the new go-to stablecoin in Defi?
To answer the question, let’s look at share of daily volume flow in/out of stablecoins since Mar 1, 2023 to date.




                                                





                                                



During depeg (Mar 11 - Mar 13), USDT was in the trend of closing the gap with USDC in daily volume share:

as low as 6% gap in swap-from volume,
as low as 8% gap in swap-to volume.

However, after depeg, USDC has regained its dominance.

title: Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity
text:
At high level, the battle of crypto exchanges consists of 2 parts: Liquidity and Trade. To service a trade, there must be liquidity. Competing for liquidity is the first half of the battle.
To better understand the battle of crypto exchanges, in this post, I will examine that first half, with focus on Uniswap and top centralized exchanges (CEX).
First, let’s have a quick lookback on some of key events in crypto exchange history.
It’s been 14 years since the Bitcoin’s genesis block mined, 13 years since the first CEX founded, and almost 4 years since the first DEX founded. Before progressing to the end of this post, some data points to take note:

title: Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity
text:
Total Assets
In this section, let’s look at total asset deposited in DEX (i.e. TVL, value locked), and in CEX (i.e. proof of reserves).


DEX total assets is roughly 15% of that on CEX, which has surpassed $100B.



                                                


source: defillama.com.
Defillama tracks 842 DEXs. Top 14 DEXs account for 81% of total assets on DEX, each have at least $100M in total assets, the largest one is Uniswap with $4B.



                                                


source: defillama.com.
Defillama tracks 28 CEXs. Top 21 CEXs which have proof of reserves (POR) range between $100M and $60B. Top 3 CEXs account for almost 80% of total CEX assets. Binance is the largest CEX in total assets.



                                                


source: defillama.com.
In top 10 exchanges, only 2 of them are DEX, which are Uniswap, and Curve, at 4th and 5th position respectively. Their assets are 7.5% or less of that on Binance.



                                                


source: defillama.com.

title: Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity
text:
Trades
In this section, to compare DEX to CEX, let’s look at DEX to CEX spot volume, which is monthly decentralized exchange volume divided by centralized exchange volume (as a percentage).
One and a half year after the first data point in Jan 2019, DEX to CEX spot volume first surpassed 1% in Jun 2020. Four months later, DEX to CEX spot volume skyrocketed to 16% in Sep 2020. Sixteen months later, it made a higher high to 17% in Jan 2022. And recently, in May 2023, it made all time high at 22%. That marked DEX spot volume surpassed a fifth of that on CEX.
In the first half (H1) of 2023, the DEX to CEX spot volume trend was generally up, started with 9.6%, and ended higher with 16.8%. This could be driven by the colaspe of FTX, and recent legal crack downs on CEXs around the world.
Combine with DEX to CEX spot volume of roughly 15% above, we can see that, from $1 of assets, DEX is on the trend of generating a higher $volume than CEX.


source: theblockcrypto.com.
In absolute $, spot volume of DEX and CEX’s all time high were $213b and $4.25t, both recorded in May 2021, which translates to 9% of DEX to CEX spot volume.
In H1 2023, though both spot volume on DEX and CEX have generally been down, DEX to CEX spot volume (as seen above) has been up, which implies a slower drop in DEX spot volume.


source: theblockcrypto.com.


source: theblockcrypto.com.
Dominances of Uniswap - the largest DEX, and Binance - the largest CEX, in each category are quite similar (40%-60% market share).
Overall, market share of Binance has been in a steeper upward slope than that of Uniswap.
In H1 2023, market shares of top 2-5 CEXs are more evenly distributed than those of top 2-5 DEXs.


source: theblockcrypto.com.


source: theblockcrypto.com.

title: Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity
text:
Total Assets

Current Assets Breakdown
First, let’s break down assets by chain, then by token to understand more about these exchanges total assets.

Chain Breakdown
All Uniswap assets are on EVM compatible chains (EVM). Of $100 worth of assets, almost $90 on Ethereum, $7 on Arbitrum, $2 on Polygon, $1 or less on each of Optimism, BNB, and Celo.



                                                


source: defillama.com.
Meanwhile, on CEXs:

Binance assets are more evenly distributed among 4 chains: Bitcoin, Ethereum, Tron, and BNB, which account for roughly 90% of total assets. The other 10% is allocated among 10 other chains.
On OKX and Bitfinex, assets on Bitcoin and Ethereum dominate heavily, account between 95% and 98% in total assets.




                                                


source: defillama.com.
Next, let’s breakdown the exchanges assets by token.


Token Breakdown
Uniswap assets allocate in more than 2k tokens. Of $100 worth of assets, $80 in top 9 tokens, of which almost $40 in WETH, $25 in stablecoins (USDC, USDT, DAI, and FRAX), almost $4 in WBTC.



                                                


source: defillama.com.
Mean while, CEX assets are less diversified in number of tokens (less than 100).

On Binance, of $100 worth of assets, about $60 roughly equally shared between BTC and USDT, $14 in BNB, $11 in WETH.
On OKX, of $100 worth of assets, $96 in top 3 tokens: USDT, BTC, and WETH.
Similarly, on Bitfinex, of $100 worth of assets, $97 in top 3 tokens: BTC, WETH, and LEO. If you haven’t heard of LEO, you are not alone 🤣.




                                                


source: defillama.com.
In absolute $, assets in WETH on Uniswap is at the level of OKX and Bitfinex ($1B-$2B), and a fourth of that on Binance.
In percentage of total assets, WETH and stablecoins have the opposite position. On one hand, assets in WETH on Uniswap is higher than all of those on the 3 CEXs. On the other hand, Binance and OKX allocate more share to stablecoins, 25% to 2 times higher than that on Uniswap. Surprisingly, on Bitfinex, stablecoins have less than 1% of total assets.
Next, let’s examine the trend of total assets in H1 2023.



Assets in H1 2023
In H1 2023, on Uniswap:

total assets started with $3.2B, and ended with $4B, or 25% increase. It ranged between $2.8B and $4.4B, or max 12% down, and 38% up (peak, trough vs. start).

Meanwhile, on CEXs:

Binance total assets started with $54B, and ended with $58B, or 7% increase. It ranged between $54B and $71B, i.e the start is the trough, and max 31% up.
OKX total assets started with $6.4B, and ended with $10.3B, or 60% increase. The start is also the trough, and the end is also roughly the peak. These imply a strong uptrend.
Bitfinex total assets started with $6.3B, and ended with $9.9B, or 57% increase. The trend is similar to that of OKX.




                                                


source: defillama.com.
Next, since (1) Uniswap only supports EVMs, and (2) EVMs onchain data is more widely supported by analytics platforms such as Dune, let’s dive into EVMs onchain data to understand more about assets flow and assets provider behavior in H1 2023.

title: Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity
text:
EVM Assets
First, let’s compare assets flow in/out of the exchanges to see if we can extract any insights.

Assets Flow

In/Out
Note: Total assets moved = total assets added + total assets removed
In H1 2023, on Uniswap:

Assets providers moved between $250M (on May 4) and $15.7B (on January 12) worth of assets in total per day. Total assets moved per day is mostly below $6B.
And the providers are noticably more active in Q1 than Q2. One possible reason is APY of Uniswap pools were higher in Q1 than that in Q2, as seen in the Median APY chart of Uniswap V2 and V3 in H1 2023 below.






                                                


source: defillama.com.
Meanwhile, on CEXs:

Binance assets providers moved $6B (on February 13) or less worth of assets per day. Total assets moved per day is mostly below $2B. The providers are noticably more active between the second half of February and the first half of March.
OKX assets providers moved $1.7B (on March 11) or less worth of assets per day. Total assets moved per day is mostly below $300M. The providers are noticably more active between the second and fourth week of March.
Bitfinex assets providers moved $622M (on March 24) or less worth of assets per day. Total assets moved per day is mostly below $200M. The providers are noticably more active in March and June.







Have you noticed that the range of total assets moved per day on Uniswap was much larger than those on the CEXs, especially relative to total assets? With this data point, one may conclude that total assets on Uniswap fluctuates more wildly than those on the CEXs. However, it’s not true as seen above, and also is demonstrated by net assets inflow in the next section. The possible reasons, in my opinion, are:

Uniswap assets providers usually move assets between pools on Uniswap, i.e. internal movement. This could be driven by pools APY.
Meanwhile, on CEXs, assets provider usually move assets, on the way in, to either sell and cash out to fiat, or deposit as margin to leverage trade, and on the way out, to either store in self custody wallets, or participate in Defi.
A caveat of storing assets on CEXs, which Uniswap and other DEXs don’t have, is assets remove/withdraw suspension. It happens due to various reasons, and different times. And the most common time is around FUD events, which raises questions among crypto community.
And finally, CEXs limit the amount of assets per withdraw, while Uniswap does not. This can be observed in the charts of assets moved per below.

In H1 2023, on Uniswap, total remove assets per transaction




Net Inflow
The net assets inflow per day of Uniswap was much smaller than that of Binance, which ranged between -$1.1B and $1.4B, and was in a similar range (between -$200M and $200M) with those of OKX and Bitfinex, despite a much higher range of total assets moved.











Assets Provider
In H1 2023:

There is a big gap between active providers per day of Uniswap (and the other 2 CEXs) and that of Binance.
Binance active providers per day started with 54.7K, and ended higher with 123.6K. The number mostly ranged between 60K and 180K, along with a few huge surge to as high as 712.5K (on May 19).




Uniswap active providers per day started with 4.9K, and ended higher with 5.8K. However, the trend in Q2 was actually down as it increased and peaked in late March. This strongly correlates with Uniswap pools Median APY above.
OKX active providers per day, on one hand, in Q1, was quite similar to that of Uniswap, on the other hand, in Q2, increased rapidly, ended Q2 with 30.5K, or roughly 6 times higher than that of Uniswap.
Bitfinex active providers per day was very small relative to the other exchanges, which implies that, on average, total assets of each Bitfinex assets provider are much higher than those of the other exchanges.

title: Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity
text:
Trades
In this section, I will compare Uniswap trading volume to those of Binance and Coinbase.
In H1 2023:

There is a big gap between total trade volume per day (7-day moving average) of Uniswap (and Coinbase) and that of Binance.
Uniswap total trade volume per day started with $446M, and ended higher with $885M, or 98% increase. It ranged between $446M and $4.4B (on March 16, during the recent stablecoins depeg event, which I covered in this post), or max almost 10 times up.
February 9 marks the first time Uniswap surpassed Coinbase in total trade volume per day.

Meanwhile, on CEXs:

Binance total trade volume per day started with $6.7B, and ended higher with $8.5B, or 27% increase. It ranged between $5.6B (on May 22) and $28.4B (on March 19, stablecoins depeg event), or max down 17% and max up more than 4 times (compare to the start).
Coinbase total trade volume per day has a similar range to that on Uniswap in most part of the period. However, it started with $873M, or about 2 times higher than that on Uniswap, and ended higher with $1.1B, or 26% increase. It ranged between $670M (on May 23, similar to Binance) and $2.5B (on January 18). One interesting data point is, unlike Uniswap and Binance, Coinbase total trade volume per day did not make the highest high during the stablecoins depeg event. This might due to the fact that Coinbase tokens are mostly quoted in USD (instead of USDT on other CEXs).



source: theblockcrypto.com.

title: Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity
text:
Volume / Total Assets
In June 2023, based on TVL on June 30, 2023, for every $1 of assets, Uniswap generated $8.7 in trade volume, which is more than 2 times higher than those on Binance and OKX. This implies, as an exchange, Uniswap is more effective with its assets than Binance and OKX.

Uniswap vs. CEXs: Liquidity

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Base is a protocol, a smartcontract platform based on Optimism Stack. We can also view it as a marketplace which has 2 sides: supply and demand.
On the demand side are users, who use smartcontracts to achieve their goals in the decentralized world. And on the supply side are developers, who build those smartcontracts.
Growing a marketplace needs both sides to grow. It’s always better to grow fast, especially in the early days like an 1-month old Base.
As you can see at the title, in this document, I will examine the supply side growth of Base. I started doing this with a question in mind: “What metrics would I want to see analyzed if I was the Base team?”. The demand side is as important as the supply side, however, at the testnet phase, I think examining the supply side is more valuable.
But first, let’s briefly look at Base testnet processing capacity such as transactions, fail/success rate, and success transaction by transaction type.






Tip



All charts below are interactive which you can scroll/click/drag/hover to change scale, zoom in/out, or view detail. This is very helpful when reading charts, especially reading charts which have outliers and log scale doesn’t add much value, thus hard to see normal data points without interactive mode.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Base is a protocol, a smartcontract platform based on Optimism Stack. We can also view it as a marketplace which has 2 sides: supply and demand.
On the demand side are users, who use smartcontracts to achieve their goals in the decentralized world. And on the supply side are developers, who build those smartcontracts.
Growing a marketplace needs both sides to grow. It’s always better to grow fast, especially in the early days like an 1-month old Base.
As you can see at the title, in this document, I will examine the supply side growth of Base. I started doing this with a question in mind: “What metrics would I want to see analyzed if I was the Base team?”. The demand side is as important as the supply side, however, at the testnet phase, I think examining the supply side is more valuable.
But first, let’s briefly look at Base testnet processing capacity such as transactions, fail/success rate, and success transaction by transaction type.






Tip



All charts below are interactive which you can scroll/click/drag/hover to change scale, zoom in/out, or view detail. This is very helpful when reading charts, especially reading charts which have outliers and log scale doesn’t add much value, thus hard to see normal data points without interactive mode.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Processing Capacity




                                                
Figure 1: Total Transactions & Fail Rate Over Time



Since launched on Feb 23. Base processed mostly between 100k and 200k transactions per day and fail rate mostly less than 5%, let’s consider these numbers as baseline. Total transactions peaked on Mar 6 at 564k, and fail rate also peaked at 17%. Since then, both total transactions and faile rate have decreased to the baseline.




                                                
Figure 2: Success Transaction by Type



In total 5.4M success transactions: the shares are 73% normal (by contracts users), 24% system (by Base), 2% deploying contract (by developers).
A developer can deploy a contract several times on Base, each deploy is 1 transactions with unique contract address. Thus using deploying contract transaction numbers to evaluate the supply side growth of Base (or other EVM protocols) is not quite accurate. A more accurate way is by counting interacted contracts (i.e. contract users interacted with).






Why would developer deploy a contract several times?





A developer may deploy a contract several times for various reasons. One reason is to test the contract’s functionality and ensure that it works as intended before deploying it to the main network. Another reason is to create multiple instances of the same contract with different parameters or configurations. Additionally, deploying a new version of a contract can be necessary if there are bugs or security vulnerabilities in the previous version that need to be fixed. Finally, developers may deploy contracts multiple times for auditing purposes or to compare different versions of the same contract.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Supply Side Growth

Contracts Over Time




                                                
Figure 3: Aggregate Contracts Over Time




Deployed Contracts

count contracts at deployed time

Interacted Contracts

count contracts at first time interacted by any user

Adopted Contracts

count contracts at first time interacted by any user who is not the contract creator


Deployed contracts has been growing much faster than both interacted contracts and adopted contracts. Base currently has 130K deployed contracts, 12K interacted contracts, and almost 1K adopted contracts. Each smaller level is 10x lesser than the bigger one.


Contracts Conversion Over Time




                                                
Figure 4: Contracts Conversion Rate Over Time



The conversion rate from deployed to interacted started with 88% on Feb 23, and then dropped sharply to 28% on Feb 27. Since then, it has decreased gradually to 9% recently.
The conversion rate from interacted to adopted mostly plateau, started with 6% and recently at around 9%.
From 2 conversion rates above, it can be summarized as for every 10 deployed contracts, only 1 got interacted by any user, and for every 10 interacted contracts, only 1 got adopted by normal users.
To close the big conversion gap from deployed to interacted to adopted, it needs more effort growing the demand side of the Base Growth team.






Note



You might wonder why there are 117K deploy contract transaction, but total contracts deployed is 124K. The reason is 1 deploy contract transaction may have more than 1 contract deployed.




Developers Over Time






Note



With developers, I don’t count interacted level because interaction of a developer with their own contracts doesn’t add signal to their contracts true adoption.






                                                
Figure 5: Aggregate Developers Over Time




Deployed Devs

count developers at the time of deploying their first contract

Adopted Devs

count developers at the first time any user interacts with any of their deployed contracts


Similar to contracts, developers who deploy contracts grow much faster than those have contracts adopted by users. As of Mar 24, Base has 37K deployed developers and 282 adopted developers.


Developers Conversion Over Time




                                                
Figure 6: Deployed to Adopted Developers Conversion Rate Over Time



The conversion from deployed to adopted started at 3.7% on Feb 24, then dropped sharply to 0.7% on Mar 01. Since then, it has fluctuated between 0.7% and 1.1%. In other words, for every 100 developers, only 1 sees their work used by normal users.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
section: Relationship between Activeness and Contracts of Developers
text:
Relationship between Activeness and Contracts of Developers

Age and Deployed Contracts




                                                
Figure 7: Age and Deployed Contracts (log)




Age

days since the first time deploying a contract


Except for a few outliers, the general trend is the older a developer, the more deployed contracts.
The most common [Age - Deployed Contracts] cluster is [2 weeks old - 1 deployed contracts] which has 9.7K developers.
One 3 weeks old developer has deployed more than 6K contracts or around 300 contracts per day. This is either a super coder, or an AI, or a team using the same address to deploy contract!


Deploy Frequency and Adopted Contracts




                                                
Figure 8: Deploy Frequency and Adopted Contracts




Deploy Frequency

deployed contracts per day = deployed contracts / age


This section is where interactive mode of a chart shines. If you zoom in the cluster at the bottom left corner, you will see that most developers have less than 10 adopted contracts, and they deploy less than 3 contracts per day.
When the deploy frequency is less than 1 contract per day, it looks like there is a positive correlation between deploy frequency and adopted contracts. However, the correlation fades when deploy frequency surpasses 1 contract per day.
There are 2 outlier developers which have quite opposite results, one deploys 26 contracts per day and has 400 adopted contracts, the other deploys 53 contracts per day and only has 9 adopted contracts.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Base Guild.XYZ




                                                
Figure 9: Base Guild.XYZ Members



A quick introduction to Guild.XYZ: Guild is a platform that allows users to create private gated communities. Base team created the Base on the same day of the testnet launch to manage and grow their community. To join the guild, each member must acquires a role by completing some actions.
You can read more about each role here
What I want to focus on is the Shadowy Super Coder role, who stars Base github repository (i.e. the developers). Currently, there are 25K of them and we know in the previous section that Base has around 36K developers who have deployed contracts on Base, which means only 2/3 of Base developer base are aware of the Base Guild on Guild.XYZ.
The goal of having a guild is to build and grow the community of both sides: Demand and Supply. The Base growth team should dig deeper into this and find a way to onboard more developers to the Guild.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Final Thoughts
On the surface, Base supply side seems to be growing very fast with both number of developers who deploy contracts and number of deployed contracts are increasing rapidly. However, taking a closer look, we can see that true usage which represented by number of adopted developers and adopted contracts (i.e used by normal users) is relatively small. Improving the conversion from deployed to adopted is the question for the growth team at Base to answer and I hope this article has provided some insight into which questions should the team ask.

title: Base’s Supply Side Growth
text:
Acknowledgments

Thanks to Flipside for being a reliable and powerful source of data. If you want to analyze crypto data, give Flipside a try.
Thanks to doc for helping me comprehending the Base tables in Flipside.

title: Uniswap and The Rise of Meme
text:
As of Jun 4, 2023, there are 856 meme coins listed on Coinmarketcap.

What is a meme coin?

A meme coin is a cryptocurrency that doesn’t take itself as seriously as leading digital currencies like Bitcoin and Ethereum. Dogecoin (DOGE) - widely regarded as the first meme cryptocurrency.
A meme coin doesn’t need to have a purpose that is as closely tied to their underlying value, like Bitcoin and Ethereum. A meme coin often seems to have the sole purpose of “going to the moon,” a phrase popularized by Dogecoin’s skyrocketing price in early 2021.


Started on Apr 17, interest in meme coins surged.



The Rise of Meme


Since Apr 17, Uniswap V3 daily volume peaked on May 6 with $1.2B. And by Jun 4, the aggregate volume is $31B.


Top 5 coins with the most TVL are WETH, USDC, USDT, WBTC, and DAI. Pools which consist of 2 in these coins are also top pools with the most trading volume. I call these pools major pools.
Of $31B, 12 major pools account for 76%, despite only account for 0.3% in total # pool.


There are more than 4k pools in the minor pools group. The pools of meme coins are in this group.


Pools in the minor group are mostly pairs of a minor coin with a major coin. Let’s breakdown volume of minor pools from the angle of swap from or to a minor coin to have a better sense of minor coins share in total minor pools volume.
Volume from and to PEPE is almost 1/4 of total minor pools volume.


From another angle, as PEPE led the rise of meme, almost 60% of minor pools volume is from and to minor coins which are PEPE or created after PEPE. These coins are likely meme coins.


When zoom into the group of PEPE or created after PEPE coins, which has 1420 coins, top 10 coins account for more than 80% of total volume.


With a quick check on etherscan.io, all of the coins in top 10 are meme coins. Without checking all of them, my assumption is all of these 1420 coins are all meme coins.


At its peak, meme pools acounted for 21% in total trading volume per day, more than half of total # trader per day, and total # trade per day on Uniswap V3.






Now meme coins are identified, let’s dive into the effect of the rise of meme on Uniswap V3 trading activity on Ethereum.

title: Uniswap and The Rise of Meme
text:
New Trader
Total new trader was highly correlated to new meme trader. As the share of new meme trader increased, total new trader increased. The situation was the same on the way down. During the period, meme pools have accounted for at least 54% of total new trader per day. At its peak, on May 5, meme pools accounted for 3/4 of total new trader per day on Uniswap V3.


In a few days that trading activity of meme coins surged, new trader share in meme pools rose higher than that in other pools, the largest difference was almost 3x (31% vs. 11% on May 5).


Thus, it is safe to say that the rise of meme did attract new trader to Uniswap V3.
However, it raised another question 👇.

How sticky are these meme traders?
I use retention rate to measure traders stickiness. Retention rate is % of a trader cohort return (to swap on Uniswap).
Retention rate of meme traders in the first 3 weeks is at least 1.5x retention rate of average traders. From week 4th, the former appears to drop faster than the latter. However, in the first 6 weeks, meme traders’ retention rate are still higher than that of average traders on Uniswap V3.




When they returned, what did they return for?
When meme traders returned, almost 90% of their acitivities were in the meme pools. And those activity only accounted for 60% in their total return volume.




Of 40% in total return volume which was not in meme pools, 3/4 was in pools consist of 2 in 3 most popular coins: USDC, USDT, and WETH.




How old are these new meme trader on Ethereum?
These new meme trader on Uniswap V3 have Ethereum ages range from 0 to 372 weeks old. And almost half of them are also new to Ethereum (i.e trade meme coins on Uniswap V3 within the first week on Ethereum).


Next, let’s examine activity of traders in the meme pools.

title: Uniswap and The Rise of Meme
text:
Trader of Meme
Looking at meme trading activity on Uniswap V3 by volume bucket, we can see that most of volume is in the buckets > $100K per trader. Meanwhile, most of trader is in the buckets < $1k per trader.


To get better insights, let’s break them down further.
I use IQR to group and find outliers. The result is meme traders grouped by trader types, separated by volume range, with summary table below.


There are 265k retail traders, account for 85% of total # trader, with $220M in total volume, this is only $60M higher than volume of the biggest meme trader, more on that later.
Meanwhile, there are 708 whale & blue whale traders, with $2.8B in total volume, this is more than the other 3 trader types combined.





Retail, Degen, & Chad Trader

Volume & Age
Trader’s volume & age of 3 trader groups: retail, degen, & chad are quite similar to one another. The most active traders cluster is 6 weeks old or less.
Additionally, the 20-70 weeks old traders are noticably less active than the others in term of both volume and # traders, especially in the high volume cluster of each group. These traders were onboarded from around Jan 2022 to Jan 2023, when crypto market was on the way down.










Behavior
Between degen and retail, what set degen apart from retail are both trade size (i.e. volume per trade) and trade frequency (i.e. # trade per week).


However, between chad and degen, trade size has a more important role than trade frequency in what set chad apart from degen.





Whale & Blue Whale Trader

Volume & Age
While whale traders have volume range of $876K - $5m, age range of 0 - 108 weeks old, the most active cluster has $1m - $2m in volume, and is 2 - 6 weeks old.


Blue whale 0xd985c35f566c9de55df16ade36852b0c40f4bf59 is 5 weeks old, has $156M in total volume, and is the biggest meme trader on Uniswap V3.
The most active cluster is 67 weeks old with 11 blue whales. Following by the 40 weeks old cluster with 8 blue whales. Unlike the trader types above, blue whales are more evenly distributed along the age spectrum.
Generally, the older a blue whale is the less volume traded.




Behavior
On average, blue whales’ trade size are bigger than that of whales. However, what set blue whales apart from whales is trade frequency. Top blue whales stand out by having trade frequency much higher than average.


A quick check on etherscan shows that these traders are actually bots which have a lot of MEV transactions.


Next, let’s examine blue whale traders profit to understand more about the top traders performance.


Blue Whale Profit
I only consider blue whales who had bought and sold meme coins and realized profit.
Not all blue whales are profitble trading meme. Their realized profit vary from - $1.5M to $2m, and margin vary from -16% to 36%. The most profitable trader is not in top 5 volume. And top 5 volume traders’ profits are less than $1m each, with margin of 8% or less.


None of top 10 profitable blue whale traders are new (6 weeks old or less). They are at least 17 weeks old. Most of them are not in top traders with most trade size or most trade frequency. A half of them have 2 digits % profit margin, the other half have 1 digit % profit margin (mostly 1% - 2%). Top profitable traders may not have the same trading strategies, however they are all experienced traders.




PEPE generates the most profit to traders with $3m, while blue whale traders yield the most profit per investment from trading PEEPO. Blue whale traders realized $79 profit from $100 investment in PEEPO.


Only top 10 meme coins in term of profit generate more than $100K in total profit each, and they all rank low in profit margin. This is quite predictable as most of them are top trading volume coins which deminish the profit margin.

title: Uniswap and The Rise of Meme
text:
New Trading Pool
Number of pool created per day on Uniswap V3 was up from Apr 17 to May 24 then down till Jun 4. During the period, pools of meme coins accounted for at least 70% most of the time, peaked at 97% on May 7.


Of 1847 new pools created, 1577 pools are of meme coins.



Meme Pool Breakdown
Meme coins are mostly paired with WETH. WETH has 359 pools where it is the base token (token0 - the first category), and 1k pools where it is the quoted token (token1 - the second category). In both categories, WETH leads with a large margin vs. the followings coins.
Surprisingly, PEPE is no.3 base token, not no.2. BURN is no.2 despite not in top 10 trading volume of meme coins.


Top 3 quoted coins are all major coins. PEPE is no.5, again trailing to BURN at no.4.


Another observation here about the ratio of # base pool / # quoted pool is that, on one hand, except for WETH’s ratio, which is around 1/3, the other major coins’ ratios are around 1/2. On the other hand, meme coins (at least with the top ones) have an opposite ratio, which is around 2.
In other words, for every 2 pools created where base coins are meme, another pool created where quoted token is meme. And for every 2-3 pools created where quoted coins are WETH or USDC, another pool created where base token is one of them.

title: Uniswap and The Rise of Meme
text:
Other Pool (i.e. not meme pool)
Though trading activity in other pools have been down. There was no clear adverse effects of meme pools on other pools. We even saw favorite effects as we learned above that meme coins attracted new traders to Uniswap V3 and when they returned, they also traded in other pools.
As trading activity (volume, # trader, # trade) in meme pools surged, trading activity in other pools also surged. And in those surges, other pools’ peaks lagged those of meme pools 1 day (as seen on May 5 and May 23). This could be resulted from meme traders exitting their meme position to major coins.






About liquidity, if only consider liquidity providers (LPs) actions during the period, tvl of meme pools and other pools saw correlation changed overtime. In one period, they increased together, in other period, one rose while the other fell.


The negative correlation between meme pools and other pools is easier to observe in # LPs. From Apr 17 to May 1, while the number was increasing in meme pools, it was decreasing in other pools. After that, the trends reversed.



LPs Overlap
8% of LPs in other pools provide liquidity in meme pools. It’s a small share, however, in aggregate, they have - $1.81B in net tvl, while the rest have $1.76B in net tvl. In other words, 8% of LPs have reduced more tvl than 92% of LPs increased tvl in the same period.
→ LPs in other pools who also provide liquidity to meme pools played a major role in other pools liquidity.




Those overlap LPs account for 22% in total LPs of meme pools. Interestingly, they have also been net decrease tvl in meme pools, however the amount is much smaller with - $29M.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Since the license expiry, 8 protocols have forked Uniswap V3 to create their own V3. By Apr 30, 2023:

Uniswap V3 has $2.9B in total TVL on 6 chains. The forks have $263M in total TVL or 9% of Uniswap V3 total TVL.
The biggest fork is Pancakeswap V3 with $260M TVL (99% in the forks total TVL).
Pancakeswap V3 is operating on 2 chains: BNB (87% in total Pancakeswap V3 TVL) and Ethereum (13% in Pancakeswap V3 total TVL).
On Ethereum, Pancakeswap V3 has $37M TVL, which is 1.5% of $2.4B Uniswap V3 TVL.
On BNB, Uniswap V3 has $20M TVL (launched on Mar 16, 2023), which is 9% of $223M Pancakeswap V3 TVL.



I will examine onchain data since the Expiry, from Apr 1 to Apr 30.

To examine the impact of the Expiry, I will examine Uniswap V3 performance on 2 chains: Ethereum, and BNB.
To see how the forks are doing relative to Uniswap V3, I will compare Uniswap V3 to Pancakeswap V3 on each of Ethereum and BNB chain. To make the comparison more compatible, I will exclude all Uniswap V3 users before the Expiry (i.e. imagine Uniswap V3 launched after the Expiry, the same as the fork).

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
I will examine onchain data since the Expiry, from Apr 1 to Apr 30.

To examine the impact of the Expiry, I will examine Uniswap V3 performance on 2 chains: Ethereum, and BNB.
To see how the forks are doing relative to Uniswap V3, I will compare Uniswap V3 to Pancakeswap V3 on each of Ethereum and BNB chain. To make the comparison more compatible, I will exclude all Uniswap V3 users before the Expiry (i.e. imagine Uniswap V3 launched after the Expiry, the same as the fork).

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On Ethereum


Value Locked




                                                



                                                


In the first 11 days, there were more increase LP than decrease LP. And by Apr 11, avl was - $78M. Apr 5 saw the most outflow with - $95M in net value locked.
The trend changed on Apr 12, it saw the most inflow with $107M in net value locked. The avl peaked on Apr 21 with $114M then decreased. By Apr 30, avl was $50M.
Meanwhile, only 3 of 30 days have more increase LP than decrease LP. And the trend of both increase LP and decrease LP were sligthly down.


Swap




                                                



                                                


In the first 14 days, the trend of volume was up. It peaked on Apr 14 with $1.3B. Then it decreased. By Apr 30, it was $482M.
Fee and # swapper peaked at the same time (Apr 19 and Apr 20).

Which coins drove the surge in # swapper?



                                                


The surge in # swapper was driven by meme coins such as PEPE, WOJAK, FADE, and MEMEME in their pools with WETH.


Did the meme coins help Uniswap V3 acquire more new swapper than other coins?



                                                


Yes, % new swapper (= # new swapper / total # swapper) in pools of the meme coins is 1.2x - 2.4x higher than in pools of other coins.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On BNB


Value Locked




                                                



                                                


In most days, the net value locked was positive. A big outflow day was immediately followed by a big inflow day. By Apr 30, the aggreagate value locked was $1.75M. This is much lower than what is showned on Uniswap V3 website. The reasons are:

I exclude BTCBR coin as its’ price data is wrong, which inflates value locked a lot.
The source data I use doesn’t have price data of UITP. The USDT<>UITP TVL is $76M (as shown on Uniswap V3 website), however its 24h volume is only $50. This raises a question that is this fake TVL. How can its 24h volume be so low relative to its TVL. My solution is also excluding UITP from calculating Uniswap V3 TVL on BNB chain.

Unlike on Ethereum, 28 of 30 days have more increase LP than decrease LP.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Swap




                                                



                                                


Volume, fee, and # swapper basically moved in a range. Volume and fee are more volatile than # swapper, which is healthier than otherwise, in my opinion.
Volume and fee might be affected by coins’ price volatility, which is normal in crypto. While with # swapper, we can have a clearer picture of swap usage.
On Apr 10 and 11, # swapper surged while volume and fee did not.

Which coins drove the surge in # swapper?



                                                


USDC, USDT, and WBNB are 3 coins behind the surge in # swapper on Apr 10, 11.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Summary

On Ethereum and BNB, impact of the Expiry on Uniswap V3 was small and only observable on value locked in the first few days after the Expiry. From Apr 1 to Apr 30, the general trends in value locked and swap activities were either up or plateau.
During Apr 2023, swap activities on Uniswap V3 saw a few surges. On Ethereum, the surge was driven by meme coins. On BNB, the surge was driven by stablecoins.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On Ethereum

Value Locked



                                                



                                                


From Apr 1 to Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 on Ethereum has $27M in aggregate value locked. This number can also be treated as TVL because Pancakeswap V3 launched on Apr 1.
4 days after launch, on Apr 4, Pancakeswap V3 saw the most inflow of value locked per day with $5.5M net value locked. However, value locked inflow lost momentum after that. On Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 saw the most outflow in value locked per day with - $10M net value locked.
# liquidity provider surged in first 4 days, peaked on Apr 4 with 77 LPs who increased value locked, and 44 LPs who decreased value locked. Since then, the numbers were in downward slopes. By Apr 30, the numbers were 18 increase LPs and 14 decrease LPs.
Most of the time in Apr, there were more LPs who increased their value locked than LPs who decreased their value locked.


Swap



                                                



                                                


During 2 weeks after launch, volume and fee were rising and peaked on Apr 14 with $9.4M in volume and $7K in fee. After that, the numbers decreased. By Apr 30, they are $4.5M in volume and $2.6K in fee.
Unlike volume and fee, # swapper kept rising during the period. On Apr 30, it peaked at 720 # swapper per day.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On BNB

Value Locked



                                                



                                                


By Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 on BNB has $214M in aggregate value locked. And the trend is similar to Ethereum, where net value locked per day peaked early on Apr 3, then lost momentum.
# liquidity provider (LP) also peaked early on Apr 4 with 2.3K # LPs who increased value locked, and 1K # LPs who decreased value locked. By Apr 30, the former was down to 987 # LPs and the latter was down to 747 # LPs.
Increase LPs was always greater than decrease LPs during the period.


Swap



                                                



                                                


From Apr 1 to Apr 21, volume and fee were in upward slopes. Volume surpassed $100M on Apr 10, peaked at $200M on Apr 21. After that, it gradually decreased. On Apr 30, it was $138M.
# swapper peaked 5 days later than volume on Apr 26 with 31.5K # swapper. Overall, the trend is up.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Summary
On Ethereum and BNB:

Value locked acitivities of Pancakeswap V3 saw a good start in the first 4 days. Both net value locked per day and # LP per day peaked on Apr 4. Then they decreased.
Swap activities have a healthier trend, were generally up from Apr 1 to Apr 30.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On Ethereum

Value Locked




                                                






                                                


By Apr 30, Uniswap V3 grew much faster than Pancakeswap V3 in term of aggregate lp and tvl.

almost 6x in # aggregate lps (2.8K vs. 500).
almost 4x in $ tvl ($104M vs. $27M).



Swap




                                                






                                                


By Apr 30, Uniswap V3 grew much faster than Pancakeswap V3 in term of aggregate swapper and volume.

20x in # aggregate swapper (82K vs. 3.7K).
10x in $ volume per day when comparing highest days ($106M vs. $9.5M).

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
On BNB

Value Locked




                                                






                                                


The picture is opposite on BNB. By Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 grew much faster than Uniswap V3 both in term of aggregate lp and tvl.

almost 20x in # aggregate lp (22K vs. 1.4K).
almost 40x in $ tvl ($224M vs. $6M).



Swap




                                                






                                                


Unlike the situation on Ethereum, by Apr 30, Pancakeswap V3 grew much faster than Uniswap V3 both in term of aggregate swapper and volume.

20x in # aggregate swapper (390K vs. 19K).
20x in $ volume per day when comparing highest days ($200M vs. $9.5M).

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
section: Pancakeswap V3 Users who are also Uniswap V3 Users
text:
Pancakeswap V3 Users who are also Uniswap V3 Users




                                                


More than 1/10 of Pancakeswap V3 LP on Ethereum are Uniswap V3 LP. The number is only 3% on BNB.




                                                


More than 1/5 of Pancakeswap V3 Swapper on Ethereum are Uniswap V3 Swapper. The number is only 1% on BNB.

title: Uniswap V3 License Expiry
text:
Summary
Since the Expiry:

On Ethereum, Uniswap V3 is growing much faster than Pancakeswap V3 (up to 4x in tvl and 20x in swap volume).
It’s the opposite on BNB, where Pancakeswap V3 is growing much faster than Uniswap V3 with bigger gap than Ethereum (up to 40x in tvl and 20 x in swap volume).
As Uniswap V3 user base on Ethereum is much larger than on BNB, the overlap between Pancakeswap V3 user base on Ethereum is also much larger than on BNB.

title: About
text: About this blog
